name: Begonia rajah
aboutText: Begonia rajah, described by Ridley, is a rare understory species from the humid lowland rainforests of Peninsular Malaysia — growing in deep shade on the forest floor in consistently warm, humid conditions, rooted in moist humus and leaf litter over rocky organic substrates with minimal light. Its sculptural foliage is among the most dramatically beautiful in the genus: large, strongly bullate leaves with a metallic olive-bronze to dark green upper surface bearing light green venation and banding of considerable subtlety, while the undersides are rich red to burgundy. The stout, creeping rhizome anchors the plant firmly as leaves emerge in succession. Rajah shares its Malaysian forest-floor home with the celebrated pavonina, and together they represent the very finest of what Peninsular Malaysia's shaded undergrowth offers the collector. The name 'Rajah' — Malay for ruler or king — was earned honestly.

quickFacts.light: Very low to low indirect light

morphology.venation: Light green venation and banding on metallic blade
morphology.texture: Strongly bullate, metallic sheen, rich red to burgundy undersides
morphology.variegation: None — metallic olive-bronze to dark green surface with contrasting venation banding

propagation.notes: Most rhizomatous begonias root readily from leaf sections or petiole cuttings placed on moist substrate. Keep warm and humid until plantlets emerge at leaf margins.

careGuide.substrate: Light, well-draining mix: 50% perlite, 30% potting compost, 20% orchid bark. Begonias are susceptible to root rot — excellent drainage is essential.
careGuide.watering: Allow the top half of the substrate to dry between waterings. Water at the base — wet leaves invite fungal problems. Reduce in winter.
careGuide.humidity: 50–70%. More tolerant of average indoor humidity than tropical aroids, but consistent moisture prevents crispy leaf margins.
careGuide.fertilising: Balanced liquid fertiliser at quarter strength every 2–3 weeks during the growing season. Do not feed in winter.
careGuide.repotting: Every 12–18 months in spring. Begonias like shallow, wide pots that suit rhizomatous growth. Don't bury the rhizome.

commonProblems.cause: Poor air circulation combined with high humidity
commonProblems.fix: Improve airflow; avoid wetting leaves; treat with a dilute bicarbonate of soda spray

commonProblems.cause: Overwatering, dead leaves left on plant, poor air circulation
commonProblems.fix: Remove dead foliage promptly; increase airflow; reduce watering
Move to a brighter position — begonias need good indirect light to maintain compact growth

items: Check for powdery mildew — a white dusty coating on leaf surfaces, especially in dry conditions; it spreads rapidly between plants
Inspect for mealybugs at leaf axils and the underside of stems where they hide in white cottony deposits
Rhizome begonias should show firm, healthy rhizome tissue — avoid plants with soft, discoloured rhizomes
Most begonias ship well as leaf cuttings — if buying a cutting, ensure it has a healthy petiole and no signs of rot at the cut end

fieldNotes.title: Rajah by Name
fieldNotes.author: Aroid Aaron
fieldNotes.content: From humid lowland rainforest in Peninsular Malaysia, Begonia rajah produces large, heavily patterned leaves with a distinctive textured surface and colouration that makes it among the more striking of the Malaysian specialist begonias. Rajah is simply Malay for 'king,' which the species carries with some justification — it is a large-growing, impressively leafed plant by begonia standards. Medium humidity requirements, reasonable drainage, patience; it tends to establish slowly but grows reliably once settled. Worth the upper middle tier it commands.
